content:
Actually, the spiciest part of a chili pepper is not the stem. The highest concentration of capsaicin—the compound responsible for the heat—is found in the white pith or placenta inside the pepper, which holds the seeds. The seeds themselves don’t contain much capsaicin but can be spicy because they’re in close contact with the placenta. The stem, by contrast, contains very little to no capsaicin and is not spicy.

So if you want to reduce the heat, removing the white pith and seeds is more effective than removing the stem. The stem is mostly fibrous and flavorless.

If you have any other questions about chili peppers or cooking with them, feel free to ask!
